title: Error Handling in Async Code
content:
Error handling in async Rust follows the same Result<T, E> pattern as synchronous code, but with additional considerations.

The ? operator works naturally inside async functions. You can use anyhow::Result or custom error types just like in sync code.

Spawned tasks add a layer: JoinHandle::await returns Result<T, JoinError> where JoinError indicates the task panicked or was cancelled. This means you often deal with nested Results.

try_join! is the error-aware version of join! -- it runs futures concurrently and returns early if any future returns an Err.

A common pattern is to use channels to propagate errors from spawned tasks back to the main task, especially when you cannot directly await the task result.
keyPoints: The ? operator works in async functions just like sync ones
tokio::spawn wraps the result in Result<T, JoinError> for panic safety
try_join! runs futures concurrently and short-circuits on the first error
timeout() wraps any future with a deadline, returning Err on timeout
Use anyhow for application-level async error handling
Consider error channels (mpsc) for complex error propagation from spawned tasks
